question: A 'business system' in the context of information technology refers to:
options: Only the hardware computers and servers a company owns

An integrated combination of people, processes, data, and technology that supports a business function or set of functions; examples: an ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) system integrates finance, HR, supply chain, and manufacturing into one connected system; a CRM (Customer Relationship Management) system integrates sales, marketing, and customer service data; a POS (Point of Sale) system integrates inventory, payments, and sales reporting; the key word is 'integrated' — a business system connects previously separate functions so data flows automatically and people have a complete view rather than managing separate silos; contrast with a 'tool': a spreadsheet is a tool; an ERP is a business system; the difference: an ERP manages data from many people simultaneously, connects to other systems, enforces rules and workflows, and maintains a history of all transactions — capabilities spreadsheets don't have; the purpose of a business system: increase efficiency (automate manual processes), improve accuracy (reduce human data entry), improve visibility (managers see real-time information), and enable coordination (different teams share a single source of truth)
Only custom-built software qualifies as a business system
Business systems are only relevant for manufacturing companies
explanation: Business systems vs. tools: the distinction is important for managers making technology investments. A tool (Excel, Word, email) is an individual productivity instrument. A business system (SAP, Salesforce, Workday) is an organizational infrastructure — like plumbing or electricity, it serves the whole organization and its quality affects everyone. The integration value: when a sales rep creates a quote in Salesforce, it connects to inventory data (can we deliver this?), finance systems (what are the margin implications?), and operations (when can we schedule delivery?). Without an integrated business system, each of these queries requires a phone call or email — slower and error-prone. The 'single source of truth' concept: a well-designed business system ensures everyone uses the same data — the finance team's revenue number and the sales team's revenue number are the same because they come from the same system. Without this, companies waste significant management time reconciling different versions of the same data.

question: A company manages its supply chain with a combination of: (1) Excel spreadsheets for inventory tracking; (2) Email chains for purchase orders; (3) A separate accounting system for invoices; (4) A CRM for customer orders. Each system is managed by a different team and data is manually re-entered between them. What specific problems does this architecture create?
options: Using multiple separate systems is always preferable to a single integrated system

Siloed systems create data inconsistency, manual work, and coordination failures at every handoff: the specific failure modes: (1) Data inconsistency: inventory in Excel doesn't automatically update when a customer order is entered in CRM → sales may promise delivery of items that aren't actually in stock; (2) Manual re-entry errors: each time data is copied from one system to another (CRM → Excel → accounting), there's an opportunity for human error (wrong quantity, wrong price); studies show 1-4% of manual data entries contain errors; (3) Information delays: when a customer asks 'what's the status of my order?', the customer service rep must call three people (sales, operations, accounting) to assemble the answer; this is slow and inconsistent; (4) Audit trail gaps: if a customer disputes an invoice, tracing the original order through email chains and multiple spreadsheets may take hours; a single system maintains a complete, timestamped audit trail automatically; (5) Scaling impossibility: at 10 orders/day, manual coordination is manageable; at 1,000 orders/day, it fails; integrated systems scale effortlessly; the solution: an integrated ERP (SAP, Oracle, NetSuite) manages all these functions in one system — one data entry, instant visibility, automatic connections between inventory, orders, and invoices
Manual processes are always more accurate than automated business systems
The company should add more spreadsheets to improve coordination between systems
explanation: The spreadsheet-to-ERP transition: most small businesses start with spreadsheets; they work at small scale because the data volume is manageable. Growth is the trigger: when a company reaches ~50 employees or ~500 transactions/day, spreadsheet-based coordination typically fails (too many people updating the same spreadsheet, version control chaos, growing error rates). The ERP investment payback: ERP implementations are expensive ($50K for a small company, $50M for a large enterprise) and risky (implementation failures are common). But the productivity improvement and error reduction typically produce ROI within 3-5 years. The 'one version of the truth' metric: a company that can answer 'what was our revenue last month?' in 5 seconds (system lookup) is more operationally capable than one that takes 3 days (reconciling spreadsheets from 5 departments). The Amazon operational advantage: Amazon's ability to process 300+ orders per second, track inventory across 1,000+ fulfillment centers, and promise 2-day delivery globally is entirely enabled by its integrated business systems — this operational excellence is a direct competitive advantage over competitors who couldn't build or buy equivalent systems.

question: Nike uses an ERP system that connects its product design, sourcing, manufacturing, distribution, and retail sales. When a shoe design is approved in Portland, the sourcing system automatically identifies qualified suppliers, the manufacturing system schedules production capacity, and the distribution system plans delivery timelines — all without manual handoffs. Nike's competitor uses email and spreadsheets for the same coordination. What competitive advantage does Nike's integrated business system create?
options: ERP systems only benefit large companies and provide no competitive advantage for mid-size companies

Integrated business systems create speed, accuracy, and decision quality advantages that compound over time: the Nike competitive advantage: (1) Time-to-market: a design change that takes Nike 2 hours to propagate through its supply chain might take its competitor 2 weeks of emails and coordination; in fashion (where trend windows are narrow), this speed difference directly affects revenue; (2) Error reduction: Nike's automated handoffs eliminate the transcription errors that occur when coordinators manually copy information between systems; a 2% error rate in a competitor's manual process on 10,000 SKUs = 200 SKU-level errors per cycle; (3) Decision quality: Nike's executives see real-time inventory, sales, and production data in integrated dashboards; decisions are based on current, accurate data; competitor executives see data that's days or weeks old and partially inaccurate; (4) Scaling efficiency: adding a new product line in Nike's system requires configuring a few parameters; in a spreadsheet-based competitor, it requires creating new spreadsheets, training people, and designing manual coordination processes; (5) Supplier management: Nike can share demand forecasts directly with suppliers through system integration (EDI — Electronic Data Interchange) enabling just-in-time production; the compounding effect: each of these advantages is small individually; together they create an operational excellence gap that widens each year as Nike's system improves and the competitor remains on spreadsheets
Business system complexity always increases operating costs without improving performance
All consumer goods companies use identical business systems with identical competitive outcomes
explanation: Business system as competitive moat: a company's business systems become a form of competitive advantage when they take years to build and can't be quickly replicated. Amazon's fulfillment systems (built over 25 years) give it capabilities (next-day delivery, real-time inventory accuracy, returns processing) that competitors can't replicate quickly. Walmart's supply chain system (Retail Link, built in the 1990s) gave it a decade-long information advantage over competitors — Walmart knew what was selling in each store every day; competitors updated their data weekly. The ERP implementation barrier: implementing SAP or Oracle for a large company takes 2-5 years and $50M-500M. A competitor can't copy an operational advantage built on years of system development and organizational learning overnight. The digital transformation challenge: many traditional companies are attempting to digitize their operations — but the challenge is not technology (software is available), it's the organizational change required to redesign processes around integrated systems. Nike's DTC (direct-to-consumer) digital acceleration: Nike's investment in integrated business systems has enabled its DTC strategy (selling directly through Nike.com and Nike stores rather than through retailers like Foot Locker) — the digital systems that track consumer behavior, manage personalized promotions, and integrate omnichannel inventory are the operational foundation of this strategy.

question: A company implements a new ERP system. 18 months after go-live, productivity has declined and employees are frustrated. The ERP vendor says the system is working correctly. An IT consultant concludes: 'The technology isn't the problem.' What is the likely cause of the implementation failure?
options: ERP implementation failures are always caused by software bugs and vendor underdelivery

ERP failures are typically caused by change management and process design failures, not technology: the implementation failure anatomy: the consultant's statement ('technology isn't the problem') is a consistent finding in ERP failure analyses; Gartner estimates that 55-75% of ERP implementations fail to meet their objectives, and the primary causes are: (1) Change management failure: employees were trained on the new system's features but not on the new processes the system enforces; they use the system in ways that work around its design (entering data in wrong fields, maintaining parallel spreadsheets 'just in case'), undermining the system's integration value; (2) Process design failure: companies often implement ERP without redesigning their business processes first — they configure the ERP to match their existing (broken) processes rather than adopting the ERP's best-practice processes; the ERP then automates their inefficient processes, adding complexity without improving the underlying work; (3) Data quality failure: ERP systems require clean, consistent data to function correctly; companies that migrate years of messy spreadsheet data into an ERP bring all their historical data quality problems into the new system; (4) Scope creep and over-customization: companies customize the ERP to match every existing exception and workaround; each customization adds complexity, slows the system, and makes future upgrades more expensive; (5) User adoption failure: employees who weren't involved in the design resent the system and find ways to work around it; the pattern: Hershey's $112M SAP implementation failure (1999) caused $150M in missed sales; FoxMeyer Drugs' SAP implementation failure contributed to bankruptcy
ERP implementation failures are rare and primarily caused by vendor failure
ERP systems always fail when implemented by companies with more than 1,000 employees
explanation: ERP failure case studies: the business press documents ERP failures regularly because they are expensive and consequential. Hershey's 1999 SAP failure: Hershey implemented SAP, Manugistics, and Siebel simultaneously in a 2.5-year timeline (vs. the recommended 4 years). The system went live during Halloween (peak season); order management failures meant $150M in candy orders couldn't be processed during the most critical sales period. The change management lesson: system go-live during peak season, with a compressed implementation timeline, without adequate user training and testing, is the implementation failure recipe. The Nike ERP failure (2000): a demand-planning software failure caused Nike to over-order $90M in Air Garnett shoes and under-order $80-100M of popular products; Nike CEO Phil Knight said the software 'sucked.' The actual cause: Nike's planning processes weren't redesigned to use the software correctly; the software failure was a symptom of process and change management failures. The successful ERP implementation characteristics: phased rollout (start with one function or region, expand gradually), extensive user involvement in design, process redesign before system configuration, data quality cleanup before migration, and sustained change management support.

question: A startup decides between three approaches to its customer data management: (A) Build a custom CRM system from scratch; (B) Implement Salesforce (a leading commercial CRM); (C) Use spreadsheets. The startup has 20 salespeople, $5M in annual revenue, and is growing 60%/year. Which choice best fits the company at this stage, and what criteria should guide the decision?
options: Building a custom system always produces better results than commercial software

The decision framework weighs cost, time, and fit against the company's growth stage: the analysis: Option C (Spreadsheets): appropriate for < 10 salespeople with simple sales process; breaks down at 20+ people due to version control, simultaneous editing, and reporting limitations; at 60% growth, the company will outgrow spreadsheets in 6-12 months; choosing spreadsheets delays the inevitable; Option A (Build custom): development time 6-18 months (the startup won't have a working CRM until it's grown significantly); engineering cost $500K-2M for a basic CRM; ongoing maintenance burden (the engineering team maintains the CRM instead of building the core product); competitive risk: while the startup builds CRM, competitors build their product; opportunity cost is enormous; build custom only when: commercial options genuinely can't meet requirements (rare for standard business functions); Option B (Salesforce): implementation time 4-8 weeks; cost $150-300/user/month = $36K-72K/year for 20 users; Salesforce's functionality (pipeline management, forecasting, email integration, mobile app) exceeds what the startup needs for now; scalable to 500+ users without re-implementation; the recommendation: Salesforce (or an equivalent commercial CRM like HubSpot at lower cost) is the clear choice: available quickly, scalable with growth, lower total cost than building, and lets the engineering team focus on the core product
Commercial software is always superior to custom-built business systems
Build-vs-buy decisions should be based primarily on feature preferences rather than strategic factors
explanation: Build vs. buy framework: the build vs. buy decision for business systems should consider: (1) Core competency: does this capability differentiate the company in the market? A CRM is a commodity capability; a startup's CRM is not a competitive advantage — Salesforce implementation is the same for competitors. If the capability is not core, buy. (2) Time to market: custom development takes months-years; commercial software deploys in weeks. Time has real value, especially at 60% growth. (3) Total cost of ownership: build cost (development, testing, maintenance, upgrades) vs. buy cost (license fees, implementation, training). For standard business functions, TCO of building typically exceeds buying. (4) Fit for purpose: commercial software is built for common use cases; custom software is built for specific needs. Only when the specific need is genuinely different from what commercial software supports does build become compelling. Amazon's philosophy: Amazon built its own order management, fulfillment, and customer service systems because they are core to its competitive differentiation. Amazon uses commercial software for HR payroll processing because that's not a source of competitive advantage. The corollary: every business system decision should be preceded by the question 'is this a source of competitive advantage?' If no, buy commercial software.

question: A retail company uses point-of-sale (POS) data to track which products are sold at each store. Walmart's POS data is shared in real-time with its suppliers through 'Retail Link' — a system that gives suppliers direct access to their product's sales data across all Walmart stores. P&G can see, in real-time, how many boxes of Tide detergent sold yesterday at every Walmart store in the US. How does this business system create value for both parties?
options: Sharing POS data primarily benefits Walmart at the expense of suppliers

Shared visibility creates a collaborative demand signal that benefits both parties by reducing supply chain waste: Walmart's benefits: (1) Reduced stockouts: P&G can see when Tide inventory is declining in specific stores and ship replenishment automatically — without Walmart's procurement team having to manually order; Walmart reduces the inventory management burden by shifting it to suppliers with better visibility; (2) Reduced overstock: P&G can slow shipments when inventory is building; Walmart avoids paying storage costs for excess inventory; (3) Supplier accountability: with real-time data, Walmart can see immediately if P&G is failing to maintain stock levels — supplier performance is measurable and objective. P&G's benefits: (1) Demand signal quality: P&G sees actual consumer sell-through, not just Walmart's ordering patterns; if Walmart orders 1,000 cases but only 400 cases sell to consumers in a week, P&G can see the inventory building before Walmart orders less; this prevents P&G from manufacturing products that will sit in Walmart's warehouses; (2) Production planning: real-time demand signals enable more accurate production scheduling; (3) Promotional effectiveness: P&G can see immediately which promotions are working (volume spikes at specific stores) vs. which aren't; (4) Competitive intelligence: seeing real-time consumer purchase data across 4,500 US stores is genuinely valuable consumer research. The shared value: Retail Link reduces the 'bullwhip effect' (demand signal distortion in supply chains) — a major source of supply chain cost
Sharing real-time sales data with suppliers has no supply chain management benefits
Information asymmetry in supply chains always benefits the more powerful party
explanation: Retail Link as competitive advantage: Walmart built Retail Link in the early 1990s — one of the first supplier collaboration systems in retail. The investment gave Walmart a supply chain information advantage that lasted over a decade. Walmart's competitors (Target, Kmart, Sears) didn't have equivalent systems, giving Walmart's suppliers better planning capability. The bullwhip effect (Hau Lee, 1997): each stage of the supply chain amplifies demand variability — small demand fluctuations at consumer level become large order fluctuations at manufacturer level. Real-time consumer data sharing reduces this amplification because manufacturers see actual consumer demand rather than retailer orders. P&G-Walmart continuous replenishment: in the late 1980s, before Retail Link, P&G and Walmart piloted 'continuous replenishment' — P&G managed Walmart's Pampers inventory directly, deciding when and how much to ship based on Walmart's POS data. This reduced Pampers stockouts by 40% and inventory by 30% in the pilot. The collaboration became the template for Retail Link. The power dynamic: Retail Link gives suppliers valuable information but also creates asymmetric power — Walmart can see suppliers' performance data and has evidence for supplier negotiations. The information flows both ways, but Walmart controls the system and sets the terms.

question: A healthcare company's business systems landscape includes: an EHR (Electronic Health Record) for patient clinical data, a billing system for insurance claims, a scheduling system for appointments, a pharmacy system for medication dispensing, and a lab system for test results. These systems don't share data — nurses manually copy information between them. A hospital administrator says: 'We have all the systems we need.' A consultant says: 'You have silos, not a system.' Evaluate who is right and what the integrated alternative would enable.
options: Having all required individual systems means the information technology infrastructure is complete

The consultant is correct — having individual systems without integration creates the illusion of digitization while maintaining analog coordination: the siloed healthcare systems problem: (1) Medication errors: nurses manually copying medication orders from the EHR to the pharmacy system may transcribe drug name, dose, or route incorrectly; the Institute of Medicine estimated 7,000 US deaths/year from medication errors — manual transcription is a significant cause; (2) Redundant data entry: a patient's allergies entered in the EHR must be re-entered in the pharmacy system and the scheduling system; when allergies change, all three systems must be updated manually; (3) Missing context at decision points: when a physician orders a lab test, the lab system doesn't automatically know the patient's current medications (in the pharmacy system); a test that would interact dangerously with a medication won't be flagged without integration; (4) Billing failures: insurance claims require information from both the clinical system (diagnosis codes) and the scheduling system (procedure dates and providers); without integration, billing staff manually assemble this information; errors cause claim rejections. The integrated alternative (an Integrated Digital Health Platform or HIE — Health Information Exchange): clinical orders automatically generate lab and pharmacy tasks; allergy information entered once propagates everywhere; billing codes generated automatically from clinical documentation; the administrator's error: equating 'having software' with 'having a system' — a system is defined by its integration, not by the number of applications
Having separate systems for each function is always superior to integrated platforms
Healthcare IT integration is only important for large hospital systems
explanation: Healthcare interoperability problem: the US healthcare system has been described as 'drowning in data and starving for information' — enormous amounts of patient data exists in dozens of systems that can't talk to each other. The 21st Century Cures Act (2016) and subsequent ONC (Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT) regulations explicitly require EHR systems to support interoperability — the ability to share data with other systems. The FHIR standard (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources): a technical standard for exchanging healthcare information that enables different systems to share patient data; major EHR vendors (Epic, Cerner/Oracle Health) have implemented FHIR APIs to enable integration. The Epic example: Epic's integrated EHR covers clinical documentation, pharmacy, lab, billing, and scheduling in one system for ~35% of US hospital beds. Hospitals on Epic can share patient records instantaneously across 180+ Epic hospitals through 'Care Everywhere' — a level of clinical coordination that paper-based and siloed systems can't match. The economic case: HL7 (the healthcare IT standards organization) estimates that US healthcare loses $30B+ annually to inefficiencies caused by lack of interoperability — duplicate tests, medication errors, and administrative burden from manual data reconciliation.
